macro_segments.title: The Requirement for a Kohein to Hold the Vessel During Kometz: Ground-Based Vessels and the Shulchan Analogy

micro_segments.title: Rava's Opening Position: Kometz Can Be Taken from a Ground-Based Vessel Based on the Shulchan Precedent
The Problem: After Taking the Kometz, Must the Vessel for Placing It Be Held by a Kohein?
Rava's Question: Does the Sanctification of the Kometz Require the Vessel to Be Held?
Learning the Kometz Requirement from Blood: Rava's Tentative Resolution

macro_segments.title: Kometz in Two Vessels: Minimum Amount Requirements and the Parah Adumah Analogy

micro_segments.title: The Tradition: Kometz Divided into Two Vessels—Rav Nachman Says Invalid, Rava Says Valid

macro_segments.title: Kometz in Two Vessels: Minimum Amount Requirements and the Parah Adumah Analogy (continued)

micro_segments.title: Rava's Reversal: Upon Reflection, Rava Agrees the Minimum Amount Must Be in One Vessel
The Source: Blood Must Reach the Minimum Amount in One Vessel (Parah Adumah Precedent)

macro_segments.title: The Requirement for a Kohein to Hold the Vessel During Kometz: Ground-Based Vessels and the Shulchan Analogy (continued)

micro_segments.title: Counter-Debate: Does Rabi Eliezer Really Learn Mincha from Dam?

macro_segments.title: The Blood Requirement: Minimum Amount of Dam Must Be Caught in One Vessel to Become Sanctified

micro_segments.title: Rav Tachlipa's Teaching: Less Than the Minimum Amount in Each Vessel—No Sanctification of Parah Water
The Question: Does the Same Law Apply to Blood of a Korban?
The Basis: Dipping in Blood—Two Textual Requirements (V'Taval and B'Dam) Both Necessary
Conclusion: Minimum Amount of Dam Must Be in Vessel from the Beginning

macro_segments.title: Multiple Sprinklings and Finger Remnants: Does Blood Left on the Finger Retain Validity?

micro_segments.title: Min Hadam Shebe'Inyan: Inferring That Each Sprinkling Requires Fresh Dam from the Vessel
The Braitah About Splattered Blood: Before and After Sprinkling—Different Cleansing Requirements
Resolving the Braitah: A Single Sprinkling Scenario, Not Multiple Sprinklings from One Dipping
The Parah Adumah Digression: When Finished, Wipe Hand; When Not Finished, Clean Finger on Vessel Rim

macro_segments.title: Rabbi Eliezer and the Chavitei Kohen Gadol: Can the Mincha Be Sanctified in Two Halves?

micro_segments.title: The Debate: Rabbi Yochanan—Whole Amount Required; Rabbi Eliezer—Can Sanctify in Halves Since Brought in Halves
Resolving the Apparent Contradiction: Rabbi Eliezer Learns Mincha from Mincha (Lechem Hapanim), Not from Dam
The Bread Breaking Analogy: Before Removing Spoons, Bread Breaks—Invalid; After—Invalid for Eating, Valid for Burning Spoons
Rabbi Eliezer's Logic: When the Time Comes to Remove, It's As If Removed—Applies to Defined Portion, Not to Mincha
The Textual Basis: The Pasuk Says Machatzitah (Half of It), Implying a Whole Mincha Initially, Then Divided

macro_segments.title: Partial Amounts and Intention to Add: When Does a Kli Sharet Sanctify Half Measures?

micro_segments.title: Rav's Position: Half an Isaron with Intent to Add—Cannot Sanctify (Eino Kadosh)
Rabbi Yochanan's Position: Half an Isaron with Intent to Add—Can Sanctify (Kadosh)
Rabbi Yosi's Resolution: Without Intent to Add—Cannot Sanctify; With Intent—Each Addition Becomes Kadosh Immediately
Rav's Consistency: Learning Other Mincha Rules (Oil, Frankincense) from Lechem Hapanim and Mincha Chotei
Conclusion: Rav Must Hold Like Rabbi Yochanan That the Full Measure Is Required

Learning from one area to another (Milta Milta)
